coredata.dc:title: A novel carbon dots synthesized based on easily accessible biological matrix for the detection of enrofloxacin residues
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xin'an
Tang, Ximing
Yu, Jiaxin
Ye, Heng
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: With the rise of nanomaterials in recent years, a variety of carbon dots (CDs) with good properties synthesized by chemicals have been developed. Herein, Enrofloxacin (ENR) can be detected by environmentally friendly poplar flower CDs (Pf-CDs). Based on the concept of green chemistry, the precursor material is waste poplar flower, which is an easilyobtainable plant biomass. Without any special treatment, the whole preparation process for Pf-CDs is quite simple. Meanwhile, the prepared Pf-CDs have outstanding properties such as brilliant luminous performance and good biocompatibility. The Pf-CDs are typically 6.40 nm in size, with excitation/emission wavelengths of 368/445 nm, respectively. It is worth noting that intermolecular hydrogen bonds are formed between Pf-CDs and ENR, leading to the occurrence of aggregation-induced-emission (AIE). The fluorescence signal of Pf-CDs showed a linear relationship with the added ENR concentration of 0.2–100 µM, with a detection limit of 0.076 µM. With a recovery rate of 94.8%-103.15%, it has shown its potentiality and wide application in the detection of ENR in dairy products and domestic water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Biomass
Fluorescent carbon dots
Enrofloxacin detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity to ENR and anti-interference of Pf-CDs
Detection of ENR by the Pf-CDs
Zeta potential of enrofloxacin and Pf-CDs
Performance and mechanism of Pf-CDs for ENR detection
Detection of ENR in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The structure diagram of ENR; (b) The schematic illustration of the synthesis and application of Pf-CDs.
(a) TEM images of Pf-CDs with different magnifications; (b) size distribution (statistics: 50 measurements from TEM images) of Pf-CDs; (c) XRD patterns of Pf- CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of PF-CDs; (b) high-resolution C1s peaks and fitting curves; (c) high-resolution N1s peaks and fitting curves; (d) high-resolution O1s peaks and fitting curves.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of Pf-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of ENR (Inset: The left side shows the situation without ENR, and the right side shows the situation with ENR); (b) Fluorescence enhancement degree (F-F0/F0) of Pf-CDs with various concentrations of ENR (n = 3); (c) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of Pf-CDs after different reaction time (F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of Pf-CDs, and F is the fluorescence intensity of Pf-CDs after different reaction time).
Recoveries of ENR supplemented in real samples detected by the proposed method.
Comparison of different ENR detection methods.

head.ce:title: A novel carbon dots synthesized based on easily accessible biological matrix for the detection of enrofloxacin residues

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning 110016, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sunwah International Business School, Liaoning University, Shenyang, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sunwah International Business School, Liaoning University, Shenyang, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rise of nanomaterials in recent years, a variety of carbon dots (CDs) with good properties synthesized by chemicals have been developed. Herein, Enrofloxacin (ENR) can be detected by environmentally friendly poplar flower CDs (Pf-CDs). Based on the concept of green chemistry, the precursor material is waste poplar flower, which is an easilyobtainable plant biomass. Without any special treatment, the whole preparation process for Pf-CDs is quite simple. Meanwhile, the prepared Pf-CDs have outstanding properties such as brilliant luminous performance and good biocompatibility. The Pf-CDs are typically 6.40 nm in size, with excitation/emission wavelengths of 368/445 nm, respectively. It is worth noting that intermolecular hydrogen bonds are formed between Pf-CDs and ENR, leading to the occurrence of aggregation-induced-emission (AIE). The fluorescence signal of Pf-CDs showed a linear relationship with the added ENR concentration of 0.2–100 µM, with a detection limit of 0.076 µM. With a recovery rate of 94.8%-103.15%, it has shown its potentiality and wide application in the detection of ENR in dairy products and domestic water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para:
Enrofloxacin (ENR), as shown in Fig. 1
a, is a third-generation fluoroquinolone, which is a chemosynthetic drug [1]. ENR has the characteristics of a wide antibacterial spectrum, low host toxicity, high bioavailability, and a long serum half-life [2]. Meanwhile, it has no significant cross-resistance with other antibiotics [3]. Compared with other antibiotics, ENR has strong antibacterial activity against almost all aquatic animal pathogens [4]. Benefiting from the advantages listed, ENR has been widely used in animal husbandry and aquaculture. Fluoroquinolones have been approved for use in animals since the late 1980 s, with ENR being the first to be approved [5].

On the other hand, concerns over the fluoroquinolone resistance of bacteria and its impact on the environment have arisen in recent decades [6]. In fact, in the body, only a small amount of ENR is absorbed, while most of it will be directly discharged from the body without metabolism. A large part will be directly discharged into the surrounding environment with livestock manure [7]. As a result, the residue of ENR in environmental water is increasing as time goes by. Meanwhile, with the abuse of ENR, some livestock has also been affected, which directly leads to the increase of ENR residues in relevant dairy products. After long-term consumption of dairy products, ENR will accumulate in the body and harm human health.

Given this situation, limiting the use of fluoroquinolones is not the only way to solve the problem [8]. It also takes a lot of effort to develop convenient and efficient detection methods. So far, various assay techniques have been opened up, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)—fluorimetry [9], HPLC—UV [10], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [11], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [12], etc. Although the methods described above are mature, efficient, and accurate, their implementation is complex, time-consuming, and costly. They are not suitable for large-scale sample detection. Therefore, it is unavoidable to open up an economic, fast, and efficient detection method to detect ENR residues in dairy products and domestic water.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of advanced optical device that uses photoluminescent (PL) carbon-based materials. CDs are spherical, and their size is less than 10 nm [13,14]. Fluorescent CDs, as an auspicious fluorescent inquest, exhibit numerous exceptional advantages over classic semiconductor quantum dots, such as accomplished water solubility, acceptable biocompatibility [15], and appealing photostability. Thanks to these advantages, fluorescent CDs have been widely used in biosensing [16] and bioimaging [17]. Meanwhile, it is also suitable for trace detection of metal ions and pharmaceuticals [18]. Sabah.H et al. designed a smartphone-assisted detection integrated with paper-based analytical device (PAD) functionalised with green emissive carbon dots (g-CDs) for on-site selective detection of Fe3+ ions [19]. Guo et al. designed the nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs)structure in the detection of ENR [20], and the method achieved great success. In addition, carbon dots also play an indispensable role in the detection of some important clinical diagnostic markers. Azad H. et al. reported a yellow emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) with ophenylenediamine (oPDA) as a precursor for the detection of bilirubin [21]. Therefore, fluorescence sensors based on carbon dots have good prospects in the trace analysis of various compounds and metal ions.

In recent years, the development and application of biomass have aroused the interest of researchers in all fields. Biomass exists widely in nature and is easy to obtain. Up to now, researchers in various fields have tried to use different biomass as a carbon source to synthesize CDs and achieved great success, including garlic [22], lemon juice [23], human fingernails [24], carp roe [25], fish scales [26], etc.

Nanomaterials synthesized with plants as precursors usually have the characteristics of low cost and low toxicity, Yousef et al. reported a new nano-fertilizer derived from Paulownia on some morpho-physiological traits of the green basil plant under drought stress conditions to evaluate the possibility of reducing nutrient uptake problems from the soil by the plant under water-scarcity conditions [27]. Ge et al. developed a fluorescent nitrogen–doped carbon dots (N–CDs) by utilizing fresh tea leaves for selective Fe3+ ions detection and cellular Imaging. Plant-derived biomass has a good prospect as a precursor material for the synthesis of nanomaterials [28]. Waste poplar flowers are very familiar in northeast China. As the precursor material for the synthesis of CDs, waste poplar flowers are easily obtained. Hence, the CDs using hydrothermal synthesis with waste poplar flowers were successfully synthesized. On the one hand, without any doping process, the whole preparation process is quite simple. Pf-CDs can be prepared by using only water and waste poplar flowers without any special pretreatment. On the other hand, the prepared Pf-CDs exhibit high intensities of fluorescence and good water solubility. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs has a well-defined linear relationship with the concentration of the added ENR within a certain range. The fluorescence of Pf-CDs is significantly enhanced due to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds formed between Pf-CDs and ENR. Based on this principle, a low-cost detection method using environmentally friendly Pf-CDs was established to determine ENR in dairy products and domestic water ( Fig. 1 b).
(Details are in Supplementary Material S1 ).
(Details are in Supplementary Material S2 ).

In the first step, waste poplar flowers collected outdoors were dried and then ground into powder. 0.40 mg waste poplar flower powder was dispersed in 20 mL purified water; The heating process was then completed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for 10 h at 180 °C [29]. After that, the autoclave is cooled. The product obtained from the autoclave was filtrated by centrifugation at 4200 rpm for 15 min and the supernatant was extracted. The supernatant was microfiltered with a 0.22 μm microtiter and then transferred to a dialysis bag filled with deionized water for dialysis treatment; the duration of dialysis was 24 h. Finally, the light-yellow solid product was obtained by lyophilizing the obtained liquid. The solid powder was dispersed in demineralized water to obtain a solution with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL for follow-up experiments.

Another essential factor to assess the function of Pf-CDs was selectivity. Considering that the actual samples may contain other antibiotics and various ions, it is necessary to investigate whether the Pf-CDs have good selectivity to ENR. Hence, the interaction of numerous ions and other antibiotics with Pf-CDs was evaluated. Firstly, the fluorescence signal of the Pf-CDs with and without other antibiotics and ions was compared. Furthermore, the interference of other antibiotics and ions with Pf-CDs was also investigated. Various sorts of metal ions (Ca2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Li2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+) and antibiotics (Enoxacin, Streptomycin, Cefadroxil, Gentamicin, Sulfadiazine, Trimethoprim, Mezlocillin Sodium, Tetracycline hydrochloride) were elected for the selectivity research. The above experimental procedure was carried out at room temperature and pressure with a fluorescence excitation wavelength of 368 nm.

The dairy products were bought in a regional supermarket, while tap water was collected in the laboratory. The protein in milk was precipitated by adding acetonitrile, and then the mixture suspension was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The resulting supernatant was centrifuged at 4200 rpm for 15 min and then filtrated through a 0.22 μm filtration membrane. The treatment process of primeval tap water was the same without adding acetonitrile. Three different concentrations of ENR (20, 40, and 80 µM) were mixed with the above-treated samples, and then the fluorescence signal was detected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Finally, the recovery rate was calculated according to the enhancement degree of fluorescence and the linear regression equation of the day. The excitation wavelength used in the detection process was 368 nm.

The cytotoxicity test based on previously reported experimental techniques was carried out to evaluate the biocompatibility of the prepared Pf-CDs [30]. The influence of Pf-CDs on cell survival rate was determined by the CCK-8 assay. The overall survival of Hepa1-6 (Mouse Hepatocellular Carcinoma Cells), MEF (Mouse Embryonic Fibroblast), and HCT-116 (Human Colon Cancer Cell) were measured in the presence of Pf-CDs. Hepa1-6, MEF, and HCT-116 were incubated for 48 h in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM) at 37 ℃with 5% CO2. 100 μL of different concentrations of Pf-CDs solution were mixed with the cell culture fluid and incubated for 48 h. Parallel experiments should be performed three times for each sample, and the control group did not contain Pf-CDs. Each hole received 10 μL of CCK-8 reagent. The hole was fixed in a thermostat. The incubation time was 4 h, and the incubation temperature was 37 ℃. Eventually, the absorbency was measured at 450 nm.

The particle morphologies and diameter allocations of Pf-CDs are shown in TEM images ( Fig. 2
a). The elemental composition of Pf-CDs was investigated by XPS. There was a good dispersion of Pf-CDs with diameters ranging from 3.0 to 5.4 nm ( Fig. 2 b). According to an evaluation of 50 random samples, the average diameter was 4.2 nm. Fig. 2 c shows the X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of Pf-CDs. A strong diffraction peak can be seen at almost 2θ at 23.5◦ from the pattern, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite. Meanwhile, the QY (quantum yield) of Pf-CDs measured in the water was 3.4% (details are in Supplementary Material S3
). As a next step, the elemental composition and chemical bonds of Pf-CDs were determined by XPS. In Fig. 3
a, elemental analysis was performed using a full-range XPS scan. Carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen were represented by three peaks of 284.81, 531.61, and 399.65 eV, and the content of the elemental composition is 75.29 %, 19.81 %, and 4.89 %. Based on the spectrum shown, Pf-CDs consist of only C, N, and O elements, indicating that their carbon skeletons have been modified with oxygen and nitrogen groups. In the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s ( Fig. 3 b), three feature peaks were documented at 284.7, 285.0, and 287.5 eV, showing the existence of C-C/C = C, C-N, and C = O in Pf-CDs [31–33]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s reveals the major peaks of C-N-C and N-H [34,35], which correspond to 399.5 and 400.1 eV, respectively ( Fig. 3 c). Simultaneously, three peaks were found in the high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s at 532.6, 531.9, and 531.2 eV, which were related to the HO-C = O, C-OH, and –OH groups [36] ( Fig. 3 d). The above studies showed that the presence of a high number of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of Pf-CDs made Pf-CDs highly hydrophilic and extremely stable in dispersion in aqueous solutions.

The optical properties of Pf-CDs were defined by UV–vis, fluorescence spectrophotometers, and FT-IR. Pf-CDs solubilized in pure water show yellow patterns under sunlight, while under the irradiation of an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm), they show strong blue fluorescence. In the UV–Vis spectrum, the aqueous solution of Pf-CDs had an absorption peak near 279 nm ( Fig. S4 a). This was ascribed to the n-π* transition of the C = O bond. As can be seen in Fig. S4 b, the excitation wavelength dictated the intensity and location of the emission peak. When the excitation wavelength was between 300 nm and 440 nm, with the increase in excitation wavelength, the peak position of the emission peak gradually moved to the long wavelength, and there was a red-shift phenomenon. The intensity of the emission peak reached its highest when the excitation wavelength was about 360 nm. After repeated tests, the optimal excitation wavelength was finally determined to be 368 nm. Subsequent experiments were performed at an excitation wavelength of 368 nm, while the maximum emission wavelength of Pf-CDs was 445 nm.

In order to confirm the types of functional groups contained in Pf-CDs, the functional groups on the surface of Pf-CDs were analyzed by FT-IR ( Fig. S4 c). The absorption peak at 3420 cm−1 was identified as the telescopic vibration peak of the N-H bonds [37]. The two intense absorption peaks near 1630 cm−1 correspond to the telescopic vibration peak of the C = O and N-H bonds [38]. The characteristic absorption band at 1385 cm−1 was identified as the telescopic vibration peaks of the C-N bonds [39]. According to the aforesaid three results, it can be concluded that secondary amino groups may exist on the surface of Pf-CDs. The presence of a C-O-C bond telescopic vibration peak was indicated by the peak at 1086 cm−1 [40]. In general, it was the presence of the mentioned functional groups that greatly improved the hydrophilicity of Pf-CDs.

Stability studies were performed to discuss the fluorescence performances of Pf-CDs under different physical and chemical environments. To verify the influence of pH on the fluorescence properties of Pf-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of Pf-CDs under different acid and base conditions (pH: 1–13). When pH changed in the range of 3 to 11, the fluorescence intensity changed only slightly. When it had an extraordinary pH, it had a lower fluorescence intensity (
Fig. S5
a). For such an obvious phenomenon, it was because some of the groups on the surface of Pf-CDs had been deprotonated and protonated in an extreme acid-base environment [41]. Although the fluorescence intensity of Pf-CDs would change significantly in an extreme acid-base environment, under normal pH conditions [42], even if the pH changed partially, the fluorescence intensity changed only slightly. Intermolecular hydrogen bonds formed between PF-CDs weaken the association of carboxylate and proton under strong acid conditions; they also weaken the dissociation of carboxyl and α-H in β-dicarbonyl groups under strong alkali conditions, slowing structural form transitions in PF-CDs versus pH and eventually decreasing fluorescence intensity changes [41]. Moreover, different concentrations of sodium chloride solutions were also prepared to investigate the fluorescence degree of Pf-CDs under different ion strengths (
Fig. S5
b). The illustration clearly showed that the fluorescence degree of PF-CDs did not change much in a certain range of sodium chloride concentration, indicating that PF-CDs are extremely stable to high ion strength. To investigate the effect of UV irradiation time on Pf-CDs' fluorescence degree, Pf-CDs solution was irradiated with a UV lamp for 90 min. After 90 min of irradiation, the fluorescence of Pf-CDs could still maintain more than 90% of its original fluorescence, which proved that Pf-CDs have outstanding resistance to photobleaching. The above results indicated that the Pf-CDs synthesized from waste poplar flowers have excellent fluorescence properties under extreme conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity to ENR and anti-interference of Pf-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Given the importance of selectivity in the design of the fluorescence sensor for PF-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs was detected under the condition of relevant ions using a fluorescence spectrophotometer to assess the overall system's selectivity. The ions involved included Ca2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Li+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, and Ni2+. Moreover, the experiments on common antibiotics including, cephalosporins, quinolones, penicillin, aminoglycosides, tetracycline and sulfonamides, are also under consideration. Only when ENR is added does the fluorescence degree of PF-CDs increase significantly in all tested objects. After mixing other test substance with Pf-CDs, the change of fluorescence intensity of the whole system is far less than that of the system after adding ENR (
Fig. S6
a & b). Moreover, considering other antibiotics and ions in the actual sample may produce an effect on the overall fluorescence degree of the whole sensor, to further prove its good anti-interference properties, it was necessary to investigate whether the fluorescence intensity of the whole system would change in the presence of other ions and antibiotics. Therefore, anti-interference experiments were also conducted. In the presence of ENR at a certain concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the whole system was investigated after adding a high concentration of different kinds of ions, including Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Na+, and K+. The results indicated that the added ions would not significantly change the fluorescence intensity of the whole system (
Fig. S6
c). In addition, the situation with antibiotics was similar. Some other classical antibiotics were selected for experimental investigation, for instance, ciprofloxacin (CIP), streptomycin (STR), cefadroxil (CEF), gentamicin (GEN), sulfadiazine (SUL), trimethoprim (TMP), mezlocillin sodium (MEZ), etc. Surprisingly, the addition of a high concentration of other antibiotics did not change the fluorescence intensity of the whole system (
Fig. S6
d). The concentration of ENR in the mixed system was 50 μM, while the concentration of other antibiotics was 500 μM. The concentration of metal ions added is 5 mM. As a result, it can be concluded that Pf-CDs had excellent selectivity to ENR and were not easily influenced by other substances in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ENR by the Pf-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The lyophilized Pf-CDs were dispersed in purified water, and the final concentration of the obtained solution was 0.1 mg/mL. After that, 0.15 mL of the above solution was mixed with an ENR test solution of different concentrations to obtain the detection system. The fluorescence signal of the whole system was measured for linear investigation, the excitation wavelength was 368 nm. The scope of the linear investigation was from 0.2 to 100 µM. The fluorescence of the system showed an increase with the increasing amount of ENR addition, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of the whole system was positively related to the ENR addition in the range of 0.2–100 μM ( Fig. 4
a). Thus, the degree of fluorescence enhancement (F-F0/F0) had a good linear relationship with the concentration of ENR added. In this case, the regression equation was y = 161.61x + 1541 and the correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.9981 ( Fig. 4 b). The detection limit (3σ/k) was 0.076 µM according to IUPAC regulations. Besides, as shown in Table 2, analysis parameters including the detection limit and linear range were even better than in previous analytical methods. There is no doubt that ENR could be detected specifically and sensitively by adding Pf-CDs to the detection system. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the whole system within 10 min after the mixing of Pf-CDs and ENR was investigated. It can be seen from Fig. 4 c that the change in fluorescence signal of the sensor within 10 min was almost negligible, which showed that the time required for the fluorescence sensor to be established to work was quite short. In other words, the mixed solution composed of Pf-CDs and ENR can be put to use after vortex treatment. As a result, the time cost of the whole detection process was greatly reduced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential of enrofloxacin and Pf-CDs
Performance and mechanism of Pf-CDs for ENR detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence analysis of ENR was evaluated by recording the fluorescence signal of Pf-CDs and ENR molecules. In the case of an excitation wavelength of 368 nm, Pf-CDs and ENRs have distinct fluorescent signals at 445 nm and 448 nm, respectively. With the increase in ENR concentration, its fluorescence intensity changed weakly. However, after adding Pf-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of the whole system of Pf-CDs and ENR changed greatly with the rise in ENR concentration. To prove that the interaction between Pf-CDs and ENR enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the whole system, the following experiments were carried out: As illustrated in Fig. S8 , the fluorescence signal of the whole system with Pf-CDs and ENR was compared with the fluorescence signal of different concentrations of ENR. It was discovered that the difference increased with increasing ENR concentration rather than being a fixed value. The concentration of Pf-CDs added in this process was fixed. Therefore, it can be speculated that the existence of Pf-CDs had a sensitizing effect on the quantitative detection of ENR. In other words, as long as enough Pf-CDs were added, the quantitative detection of ENR even at lower concentrations could be realized. As for how Pf-CDs and ENR worked, it can be inferred that intermolecular hydrogen bonds were formed between ENR and Pf-CDs. The formation of hydrogen bonds limited the non-radiation channels of Pf-CDs. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of Pf-CDs increased significantly. In short, the formation of hydrogen bonds between PF-CDs and enrofloxacin led to aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [45]. According to the analysis of infrared spectrum and XPS data results, the –CONH- group was successfully modified on the surface of Pf-CDs. Through the comparison of infrared spectra, it can be found that after adding ENR, the stretching frequency of the –NH group of Pf-CDs shifted up by 21 cm−1, which was consistent with the results previously reported by Nataliya et al. [46], proving the formation of hydrogen bonds on –NH- ( Fig. S9
a). For further explanation, acetone, DMF (N, N-dimethylformamide), ethanol, and methanol were selected to investigate the changes in the fluorescence emission spectra of Pf-CDs in different solvents. Acetone and DMF were aprotic solvents. Methanol, ethanol, and water were proton solvents [47] ( Fig. S9
b). Surprisingly, in the different solvents listed above, there was no significant red shift in the fluorescence emission peak of Pf-CDs [48], and the fluorescence intensity of Pf-CDs did not increase with the increase in solvent polarity. This result ruled out the possibility of solvent-induced fluorescence enhancement. In aprotic solvents, because DMF also had C = O and C-N groups that could be used as hydrogen bond receptors, the fluorescence intensity of Pf-CDs in DMF was slightly higher than that of Pf-CDs in acetone, which had only C = O groups. In proton solvents, the electron repulsion effect of ethyl contained in the ethanol structure was stronger than that of methyl contained in the methanol structure, resulting in the electron cloud density of the oxygen atom in ethanol structure being higher than that of methanol and water. Therefore, ethanol accepted protons more easily than the other two. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of Pf-CDs in the ethanol was significantly higher than that in water and slightly higher than that in methanol. Thus, it can be inferred that –CONH- tended to act as a hydrogen bond donor in the process of hydrogen bond formation, and the fluorescence intensity of Pf-CDs in different solvents would be positively correlated with the proton acceptance ability of the corresponding solvent. Considering that there were such groups in the structure of ENR that were easy to accept protons and form hydrogen bonds, with the addition of ENR, the fluorescence intensity of Pf-CDs would be enhanced due to the formation of hydrogen bonds with ENR, the relevant experimental results of Zeta potential can also support the formation of hydrogen bonds between ENR molecules and Pf-CDs. Therefore, it finally came to the conclusion that it was the secondary amides modified on the surface of Pf-CDs that formed intermolecular hydrogen bonds with C = O, C-F [43], and C-N groups in the ENR structure, resulting in aggregation-induced emission (AIE). These results proved that the addition of Pf-CDs had a certain sensitization effect on the quantitative detection of ENR ( Fig. S9
c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ENR in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practicability of using the Pf-CDs in actual samples, Pf-CDs were used to detect ENR in milk and tap water. ENR was not detected in milk and tap water samples; as a result, the residue of ENR in the actual samples was detected by the standard addition method. Three different concentrations of ENR solution were mixed with the milk and tap water samples. The recovery results are shown in Table 1 , and the reproducibility (RSD%) value was less than 2.62% (n = 3). In addition, some previously reported technology for ENR detection was gathered in Table 2 to compare with this work. The method used in this work had a wider sensing range and a lower detection limit. Moreover, the short reaction time also fully showed the convenience of this method. The whole operation process also showed the advantages of this method, such as low cost, simplicity, and convenience. All in all, these results showed that the synthesized Pf-CDs as fluorescent sensors had good performance to detect ENR residues in milk and tap water without being easily disturbed by the matrix. They could detect ENR residue quickly and had a good prospect for the detection of ENR residues in dairy products and domestic water.

The survival rates of MEF, HCT-116, and Hepa1-6 cells in the presence of Pf-CDs were measured to assess their cytotoxicity and biocompatibility. A CCK-8 colorimetric assay was performed on MEF, Hepa1-6 cells, and HCT-116 to assess Pf-CDs' cytotoxicity. Based on the previous experimental methods, there were different effects on cell survival rates with different concentrations of Pf-CDs solution (12.5–200 g/mL). From Fig. S10
a, even though the highest dose of Pf-CDs was added, the cell survival rates were still over 90% after incubation for 60 h. According to the t-test, there was no significant difference between the CD treatment group and the control group. By comparing the images of cells (
Fig. S10
b - Fig. S10
g), despite processing for up to 60 h, the images of cells with and without Pf-CDs did not seem to differ much. It was clear from this experiment that even high concentrations of Pf-CDs did not significantly affect cell survival rates. As a result, the Pf-CDs synthesized in this work had excellent biomedical potential.

In conclusion, using waste poplar flowers as a precursor, low-toxicity, low-cost, and environmentally friendly Pf-CDs were successfully prepared. Not only do the precursor materials for synthesizing CDs come from a wide range of sources, but the prepared Pf-CDs also have the characteristics of good biocompatibility and a simple preparation process. Moreover, the data from XPS and FT-IR proved abundant oxygen-containing functional groups existed on the interface of Pf-CDs, which was instrumental in improving solubility and fluorescence performance. Meanwhile, based on aggregation-induced-emission effect (AIEE) [45], the hydrogen bonds formed between Pf-CDs and ENR limited the opening of non-radiation channels, enhanced the fluorescence, and provided a basis for subsequent accurate quantitative detection of ENR. The detection range is 0.2–100 μM while the limit of detection is 0.076 μM. According to the results of the cytotoxicity assay, it was also found that the prepared Pf-CDs showed excellent biocompatibility. This study fully expounded on the feasibility of the synthesis of Pf-CDs based on the concept of waste utilization. In other words, it offered a novel method for the assay of ENR residues and opened up ideas for the selection of precursor materials for CD synthesis.

Xinan Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Validation, Data curation. Ximing Tang: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Jiaxin Yu: Formal analysis. Heng Ye: Investigation, Data curation. Longshan Zhao: Resources, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108690.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
